item.title: Cops vs Dude with gatling fireworks cannon
item.dc:creator: HanoverFist69

item.title: I was encouraged to post a progress pic :)
item.description: This is 500lbs to 400lb. That skirt was the first bought aside for leggings in years. I have lost over another 30lbs since the second photo but I haven’t really taken a good full body pic since then. I’m smaller than I’ve been in over 10 years. I know I’m not tiny, and not pretty. But I feel so hopeful
item.dc:creator: Littleblueshoes

item.title: Sword and sorcery movie posters
item.description:
Conan the Barbarian (1982); art by Renato Casaro
Beastmaster (1982); art by C.W. Taylor
Sorceress (1982)
The Sword and the Sorcerer (1982); art by Peter Andrew Jones
The Sword of the Barbarians (1982)
Krull (1983)
Deathstalker (1983); art by Boris Vallejo
Conquest (1983)
Hundra (1983)
Ladyhawke (1985); art by Gil Formosa
Fire and Ice (1983); art by Frank Frazetta
Conan the Destroyer (1984)
Amazons (1986); art by Boris Vallejo
Red Sonja (1985); art by Renato Casaro
Barbarian Queen (1985); art by Boris Vallejo
Highlander (1986); art by Steven Chorney
Deathstalker II: Duel of the Titans (1987); art by Boris Vallejo
Deathstalker and the Warriors from Hell (1988); art by Boris Vallejo
Willow (1988); art by John Alvin
Deathstalker IV: Match of Titans (1991); art by Boris Vallejo
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: 13 Years Sober - Mugshot vs. Today
item.description: After nearly 20 years of struggling with a wide variety of substances, I'm so grateful to have reached 13 years sober.  In my 13 years of recovery, one of the greatest gifts I've received is the sense of purpose I feel in sharing my personal experience to help those still struggling understand that recovery is real and possible.  I've spent the last 12 years working in mental health and peer support, and have had the opportunity to combine personal experience and professional knowledge to help change lives and even save lives.  An equally large blessing is that I've been sober and present for my daughter's entire life.  While she knows the story of my past, she's never had to live through any of that.  I thank God that I managed to get sober in time to give her the love and the joyful and peaceful life she deserves.  Shane Watson (Silverladder.com)
item.dc:creator: silverladder

item.title: Tidying the Meme Cupboard (pt 8?)
item.description:
He looks just like his father. He must be so proud
Sad greige nurseries are such a bummer!
They have answered, and IIRC it is to earn soup!
Derpy Loki tax! I hope everyone had a great Thanksgiving. My husband has a gastric bypass earlier this month and is still only able to eat purees (soft food stage starts Tuesday!), so we stayed home. But it was nice to just sit around and not feel rushed!
One year for my birthday, my friend wrote and signed a letter stating she wasn’t mad at me and actually did like me. I’d like to take this opportunity to shout about the benefits of therapy!
Sent from my own father, of course. He can catch ya off guard with his meme timing too lol
My husband knows this is likely the case for me, too.
Remember to tip and leave a review!
These are lofty expectations.
I remember getting this news so vividly. Who would punch Rick Moranis? He’s a gem. A goddamn delight.
That matte mousse was an undiagnosed autist’s NIGHTMARE. The worst sensory experience possible.
Remember the joy? Remember the relief? Such good times.
item.dc:creator: clevergrrl420

item.title: Ugh, some people.
item.description: Don't send that message or that email. Don't say that thing. Because if you do, that is unfortunate. This kickass lady is Leslie Horton of Global News Calgary.

item.title: My grandpa loved telling this story at the local vfw
item.description: My grandpa was a clumsy man with what would be recognized today as severe ADHD who badly injured himself at least once a year. He would go on to lose two more fingers while doing his wood working hobby. He actually was an experienced 3 year combat vet at that point and this would end up being his second purple heart.
item.dc:creator: Hadestown24

item.title: I did not see that coming
item.dc:creator: MayMyEnemiesLiveLong

item.title: Fuck Alex Jones
item.description: I've been listening to Knowledge Fight for a few years now, and I've relistened to the episodes dealing specifically with AJ's defamation suits a few times, but listening to the Robbie Parker interview is like hearing it all again for the first time. Alex Jones is a disgusting fraud. He made money off the suffering of families and the deaths of school children. Anyone who takes this fucking subhuman sack of shit seriously is my enemy, now and forever. I implore you to listen. I implore you to shut the people who like Alex Jones out of your life. They have been poisoned by this dipshit's "just asking questions" routine and in my opinion are beyond redemption. Don't waste time trying to inform them, just let them know you don't have room in your life for people who think dead kids are "actors" and their families are "Democrat puppets" trying to take their guns away.
item.dc:creator: SpoZoTheReReRisen

item.title: In the 1950s,baby strollers became an essential part of family life, especially in small towns like Opawa, New Zealand. The sight of strollers strapped to the front of buses offers a unique snapshot of a time when public transportation was vital
item.description: In the 1950s, baby strollers became an essential part of family life, especially in small towns like Opawa, New Zealand. The sight of strollers strapped to the front of buses offers a unique snapshot of a time when public transportation was more than just a way to get from point A to point B—it was an integral part of daily life for families. In Opawa, a residential suburb of Christchurch, the local bus system went beyond simply carrying passengers; it adapted to the needs of parents, providing space for strollers so that families with young children could travel with ease. This practical solution reflects the community-oriented spirit of the time, where public transportation was seen as a service designed to support families and improve accessibility for all. The 1950s marked a period of growth and recovery in New Zealand, as the country rebuilt after World War II. The post-war years saw a baby boom and a rising sense of prosperity, which were reflected in the increasing number of young families. In towns like Opawa, buses connected suburban neighborhoods to urban centers, shopping districts, and recreational spots. Accommodating strollers on the front of buses made it easier for parents to travel without the added burden of managing a stroller inside the bus. It was a practical solution to the needs of a growing family-focused society, where getting around with children in tow was made more convenient by thoughtful adaptations to public transportation. This image also offers a glimpse into the evolving roles of women in the 1950s, particularly in terms of child-rearing and maternal care. During this era, many women were still primarily responsible for household duties and the care of children, and public transportation played a significant role in their daily routines. The ability to securely strap a stroller to the front of a bus without worrying about space or maneuvering it inside made life easier for mothers who needed to manage multiple tasks. This photograph not only highlights the practical solutions designed to support families but also serves as a window into the interconnectedness of family life, community, and transportation in post-war New Zealand, where thoughtful design helped improve the quality of everyday living.
item.dc:creator: Joyika

item.dc:creator: SweepTheLeg31

item.title: Is there yolk on my face? An egg exploded
item.description: Tl;dr / speed run: Figured out I was a Transwoman at Age 30 beyond a shadow of a doubt, US hosed the election, decided to come out rather than repress it, 7-year toxic relationship ended, have been slowly coming out to people (liberal parts of family / friends / roommate / others) 1:1 except at work where we have a whole LGBTQ group that knows plus a few others. Everyone had been wholly supportive in the open, plus I had a few interactions that were incredibly affirming. Capped off the month by coming out to my first immediate family member, i.e. my mom, and girl howdy, my expectations were met. Immediate disappointment and concern (she thought I was coming out as gay, ) melded with a verbal level of support plus an indication she wasn't going to use my chosen name for her own discomfort and not my privacy. We'll see how that progresses and if she doesn't just start outing me to the rest of the family. Assuming no, next step is to tell my younger brother, and by their hopeful support combined, I can tell my incredibly conservative stepdad. Actual story: Have had a woodpecker in the back of my head for the better part of my life. It got real loud after using Generative AI to re-envision myself as the opposite gender. Seven years of a terrible relationship left me grasping for anything emotionally grounding/uplifting. After 30 years of life, I finally started exploring this repressed identity in private separate from my partner a few months ago. Thought I was going crazy and had just created the mental projection of an ideal partner as a means of still fighting the mere thought that it could be anything more. I couldn't shake the feeling and wanted nothing more than to be with this projection anytime I could have some alone time. An unrelated medical procedure laid me up in bed for several days where I couldn't have any such time, but my thoughts were completely filled with her. Between this site and others, I felt obligated to actually challenge myself and go with a "What if I were Transgender?" line of questioning. What evidence is there of that? So I started a list going as far back in my life as I could, thinking of times I openly questioned my gender or exhibited behaviors indicative of it. That list was exhaustingly long and by the end I was crying. The thought of her being something separate gave way to the understanding that it was just me. The happiness I felt shined immensely bright and heading into the election at the start of the month, my optimism for the future was at an all time high, and there was a hope that I hadn't known for at least a decade. We all know how that panned out. Stayed up the whole night with optimism warping into a full-blown all day panic attack. Started getting paperwork together to move to Canada (having discussed with my partner previously on a wholly unrelated note to the gender identity redux unfolding). By the end of the day, I could tell she was confused, and with so many absolutely saddening stories coming out of the Trans community that day, here and on other sites, I made a judgment call to embrace it and come out to her. When she saw me, she thought it was just going to be a cross-dressing kink, but the look of bewilderment on her face as I told her I was a woman was not ideal. She said she wasn't sure that she was really processing it and that she didn't want to reject me like one of her other friends had done when their partner had come out. It was the usual day of the week that I'd go check on our city apartment (quick acknowledgement of our large amount of privilege, I'm a higher level Software Engineer and do alright financially). The next day she called me and finally ended the relationship expressing that my coming out was the cherry on top and not the sole reason for it being over. I was completely unsurprised. Our next few interactions were fairly hostile towards me with accusations that I led her on and was using her as a beard for seven years (even though I just figured it out myself). Well aware that this is wholly one-sided so I won't belabor the point as to all of the other interactions around the relationship ending. That's not the point of this, although, I will say I'm still waiting to get kicked in the head by the fact it's over. The momentum from coming out seems to be staving that off even now several weeks later. In any case over the ensuing weeks, I started telling some friends (reconnecting to a few I hadn't seen since the beginning of the now ended relationship) and the more liberal side of my family who live around the corner. I've started coming out at work, and in general everyone has been immensely supportive. This gave me the courage to finally start coming out to my immediate family in rank order based on conservatism (spoiler alert, they all are), starting with my mom. That was hands down the hardest conversation of my life, literally managed to do it in 4 lines that will forever be emblazoned in my memory. "You're my biggest fan, right? What would you give for me to be happy? Would you give up your son? In exchange for a daughter?" When she hung her head, I could feel her disappointment, confusion, sorrow, and worry wholly. She said she wasn't going to give me up regardless, but she expressed her biggest concern was to how break the news to my stepdad. It felt like I was putting her in a Sophie's choice position. My confidence in her claim that she wasn't going to give me up was slightly countermanded by her openly stating that she wasn't going to use my chosen name for her own comfort, not for my privacy. She's continued to claim that the biggest weight she feels is concern that the world sucks and people are going to be terrible, including and especially my stepdad, so we'll see. Next step is to tell my younger brother and try to get them to, present my case to my stepdad? Honestly, I don't give a hoot. My mother and my brother are the only two immediate family members where rejection would make this a lot harder than otherwise. Pragmatically, everyone else in the family that comes along is almost a bonus prize at this point. All that aside, going to come out fully at work now that Thanksgiving is over. I'm one of the most respected engineers at our company, so I don't see that changing much (possibly hubris). Have plenty of friends to keep telling to keep that gravy train rolling. I'm unaware of any that are on Imgur, but if any of my old fraternity brothers ever read this, feel free to look at the username, think on what it was derived from, and you can figure out who I am before I come visit. Also, you let a girl into the fraternity :p If you made it this far, here's a consolation joke. What's a dog's favorite lettuce? A-woo-gala Terrible, I know... I'd see myself out, but I already did that once today. RIP woodpecker
item.dc:creator: MistressRoboto
